---
title: "Air Quality"
description: "How the air Canadians breathe has changed — emissions, concentrations, the international picture, and the rising challenge of wildfire smoke"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import os
from pipeline.config import CANADA_COLOR, get_data_date
from pipeline.charts import lines_over_time, peer_comparison_line, ranked_bar

# Shared pollutant palette. PM2.5 is red — it is the pollutant now moving the
# wrong way (wildfire smoke), so it is the one to draw the eye.
AQ_COLORS = {
    "SOx": "#1f77b4", "SO2": "#1f77b4", "NOX": "#ff7f0e", "NO2": "#ff7f0e",
    "VOC": "#2ca02c", "CO": "#9467bd", "O3": "#8c564b", "PM2.5": "#d62728",
}
```

Air pollution is one of the clearer environmental success stories of the past few
decades — and, increasingly, an exception. Emissions of the classic industrial
pollutants have fallen sharply since 1990, and the concentrations Canadians
actually breathe have followed for most pollutants. The conspicuous exception is
fine particulate matter (PM2.5), which has risen as wildfire smoke reaches
populated areas more often. All concentration measures here are
**population-weighted** — they reflect the air where people live, not remote
monitoring sites — and are drawn from Environment and Climate Change Canada's
national monitoring and inventory programs.

## Emissions of the main air pollutants have fallen since 1990

Canada's [Air Pollutant Emissions Inventory](https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change/services/pollutants/air-emissions-inventory-overview.html)
tracks how much of each pollutant is released nationally. The declines are large:
sulphur oxides — the driver of acid rain — have fallen by roughly four-fifths since
1990 as smelters and coal plants were controlled or closed; carbon monoxide and
nitrogen oxides have fallen by roughly half or more, largely through tighter
vehicle standards. Not everything fell: ammonia, mostly from agriculture, has
drifted up. (Shown as an index, 1990 = 100, so the *rates* of change are
comparable across pollutants of very different size.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
apei_path = "data/environment/apei_emissions.csv"
if os.path.exists(apei_path):
    ap = pd.read_csv(apei_path)
    keep = ["SOx", "NOX", "VOC", "CO"]
    ap = ap[ap["pollutant"].isin(keep)].copy()
    base = ap[ap["year"] == 1990].set_index("pollutant")["emissions_t"]
    ap["index"] = [r.emissions_t / base[r.pollutant] * 100 for r in ap.itertuples()]
    fig = lines_over_time(
        ap, "year", "index", "pollutant",
        yaxis_title="Emissions (1990 = 100)", colors=AQ_COLORS,
        group_order=keep, hover_decimals=0,
        source_note=f"Source: Environment and Climate Change Canada, Air Pollutant Emissions Inventory | Data as of: {get_data_date(apei_path)}")
    fig.add_hline(y=100, line_dash="dash", line_color="#bbb", opacity=0.6)
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("APEI emissions data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## What Canadians breathe: concentrations since 2009

Emissions are what we release; concentrations are what we actually breathe. This
indicator, built on the [National Air Pollution Surveillance (NAPS)](https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change/services/air-pollution/monitoring-networks-data/national-air-pollution-program.html)
network, tracks the population-weighted concentration of each pollutant (indexed
to 2009 = 100). Sulphur dioxide, nitrogen dioxide, and volatile organic compounds
have all declined; ground-level ozone — a secondary pollutant that forms in the
atmosphere — has been roughly flat. **Fine particulate matter (PM2.5) is the
exception: it has risen, not fallen** — the signature of wildfire smoke, which the
final chart isolates. These are national figures; to pick an individual city and
see its pollutants month by month back to the 1970s, see
**[Air Quality by City](air-quality-cities.qmd)**.

```{python}
#| echo: false
cesi_path = "data/environment/cesi_air_quality.csv"
if os.path.exists(cesi_path):
    aq = pd.read_csv(cesi_path)
    avg = aq[aq["metric"] == "average"].copy()
    order = ["SO2", "VOC", "NO2", "O3", "PM2.5"]
    fig = lines_over_time(
        avg, "year", "index", "pollutant",
        yaxis_title="Concentration (2009 = 100)", colors=AQ_COLORS,
        group_order=order, hover_decimals=0,
        source_note=f"Source: Environment and Climate Change Canada, Canadian Environmental Sustainability Indicators (NAPS) | Data as of: {get_data_date(cesi_path)}")
    fig.add_hline(y=100, line_dash="dash", line_color="#bbb", opacity=0.6)
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("CESI air-quality data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## How Canada's PM2.5 compares internationally

Against OECD peers, Canada's baseline air is among the cleanest: by mean
population exposure to PM2.5, Canada ranks **4th of the 16 peers with data** —
behind only Finland, Sweden, and Norway. (This OECD series, derived from
satellite and ground measurements, currently runs to 2020 — before the most
severe recent smoke years.) The dotted line marks the [World Health
Organization's 2021 guideline](https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240034228)
of 5 µg/m³; that guideline is strict enough that most Canadians still live above
it — roughly four-fifths of the population — even though Canada's air is cleaner
than almost every peer's.

```{python}
#| echo: false
pm_path = "data/environment/oecd_pm25_exposure.csv"
if os.path.exists(pm_path):
    pm = pd.read_csv(pm_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(
        pm, x_col="year", y_col="pm25",
        title="", yaxis_title="PM2.5 (µg/m³, population-weighted)", show_average=True,
        topical=["FIN", "NOR"])
    fig.add_hline(y=5, line_dash="dot", line_color="#2ca02c", opacity=0.8,
                  annotation_text="WHO 2021 guideline (5 µg/m³)",
                  annotation_position="top left", annotation_font_color="#2ca02c")
    fig.update_layout(annotations=list(fig.layout.annotations) + [dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Green Growth Indicators (satellite-derived) | Data as of: {get_data_date(pm_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False,
        font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("OECD PM2.5 data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

### Latest PM2.5 exposure: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(pm_path):
    pm = pd.read_csv(pm_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(
        pm, "pm25", "PM2.5 (µg/m³, population-weighted)",
        f"Source: OECD Green Growth Indicators | Data as of: {get_data_date(pm_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f} µg/m³<extra></extra>")
    fig.add_vline(x=5, line_dash="dot", line_color="#2ca02c", opacity=0.8,
                  annotation_text="WHO guideline", annotation_position="top",
                  annotation_font_color="#2ca02c")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## The new challenge: wildfire smoke

The one pollutant moving the wrong way is fine particulate matter, and wildfire is
why. This chart pairs the area burned by wildfire each year (bars) with the
**peak** PM2.5 concentration Canadians are exposed to (the 98th-percentile of
daily values, indexed to 2009 = 100). The worst smoke years stand out — 2018, and
above all the record 2023 fire season, when the peak exposure index reached nearly
three times its 2009 level. Population-weighted smoke depends on *where* fires
burn relative to where people live, so the link is not exact year to year, but the
trend is clear: wildfire smoke is increasingly the dominant influence on what
Canadians breathe on the worst days. See [Geography → Fire & Ice](../geography/fire-ice.qmd)
for the long-run wildfire record and 2023 fire map.

```{python}
#| echo: false
wf_path = "data/geography/nfdb_wildfire.csv"
if os.path.exists(wf_path) and os.path.exists(cesi_path):
    wf = pd.read_csv(wf_path)
    nat = wf[(wf["geography"] == "Canada") & (wf["year"] >= 2009)].sort_values("year")
    peak = aq[(aq["pollutant"] == "PM2.5") & (aq["metric"] == "peak")].sort_values("year")
    fig = go.Figure()
    fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
        x=nat["year"], y=nat["area_burned_ha"] / 1e6, name="Area burned",
        marker_color="#d9a441", opacity=0.85,
        hovertemplate="%{x}: %{y:.1f} M ha burned<extra></extra>"))
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=peak["year"], y=peak["index"], name="PM2.5 peak exposure (2009 = 100)",
        mode="lines+markers", line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=3),
        marker=dict(size=6), yaxis="y2",
        hovertemplate="%{x}: PM2.5 peak index %{y:.0f}<extra></extra>"))
    fig.update_layout(
        plot_bgcolor="white", height=470, hovermode="x unified",
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", dtick=2),
        yaxis=dict(title="Wildfire area burned (million ha)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   rangemode="tozero"),
        yaxis2=dict(title="PM2.5 98th-percentile peak (2009 = 100)", overlaying="y",
                    side="right", rangemode="tozero", showgrid=False),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="top", y=-0.12, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
        margin=dict(t=30, b=120, r=70),
        annotations=[dict(
            text=f"Sources: NRCan Canadian National Fire Database (area burned); ECCC CESI / NAPS (PM2.5 peak) | as of {get_data_date(wf_path)}",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.30, showarrow=False,
            font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Wildfire and/or PM2.5 data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Download Data

- [Air-pollutant concentrations, indexed (CSV)](../data/environment/cesi_air_quality.csv) — ECCC CESI air-quality indicators (NAPS)
- [Air-pollutant emissions, national (CSV)](../data/environment/apei_emissions.csv) — ECCC Air Pollutant Emissions Inventory (APEI)
- [Mean population exposure to PM2.5 (CSV)](../data/environment/oecd_pm25_exposure.csv) — OECD Green Growth Indicators
- [Population exposed to PM2.5 above WHO guideline (CSV)](../data/environment/oecd_pm25_above_who.csv) — OECD Green Growth Indicators

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## About these data

**Concentrations vs. emissions.** *Emissions* (APEI) measure what is released into
the air nationally; *concentrations* (NAPS/CESI) measure what is actually present
in the air where people live. They can diverge — for example, PM2.5 concentrations
have risen on smoky days even though directly emitted particulate emissions have
changed little, because wildfire smoke is not fully captured in the controllable
emissions inventory.

**Population-weighting.** The concentration indicators weight each monitoring
station by the population nearby, so they approximate the exposure of a typical
resident rather than a simple average across all monitors.

**International series.** The OECD PM2.5 figures are modelled from satellite and
ground observations on a consistent basis across countries (so they differ
slightly from Canada's own ground-network figures) and currently extend to 2020.
South Korea is the one peer without data in this series. All sources are published
under the Open Government Licence – Canada or the OECD's terms of use.
:::
